extra.message: User input from the 'name' query parameter is interpolated into a Jinja2 template string via f-string on line 38, then rendered with Template(content).render() on line 41. An attacker can inject Jinja2 template expressions such as {{7*7}} or {% import os %}{{os.popen('id').read()}} to achieve remote code execution. The commented-out test at tests/test_main.py:39-41 confirms this is a known issue.

User input from the 'name' query parameter is directly interpolated into an HTML string via f-string without any escaping or sanitization. Since the response is HTMLResponse, an attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript by passing name=<script>alert(1)</script>.

The response body from an external HTTP service (public_ip_response.text on line 36) is directly embedded into HTML on line 38 without sanitization. If the external service is compromised, spoofed, or the PUBLIC_IP_SERVICE_URL is redirected to a malicious endpoint, the response could contain arbitrary HTML/JavaScript that would be rendered in the user's browser.

requests.get() is called with config.PUBLIC_IP_SERVICE_URL which is read from an environment variable (app/config.py:11) with no URL validation or scheme restrictions. In containerized or shared environments where environment variables may be injectable, an attacker could set this to an internal URL (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ for cloud metadata) or file:///etc/passwd to access internal resources.

SUPER_SECRET_NAME is hardcoded as 'John Ripper' in source code. Hardcoded secrets in source code cannot be rotated without changing the code and are exposed to anyone with read access to the repository. The FIXME comment indicates this was intended to be moved to an environment variable.

SUPER_SECRET_TOKEN is hardcoded as '5u93R53Cr3tT0k3n' in source code. This secret token is embedded directly in the codebase and visible to anyone with repository access. It cannot be rotated without a code change. The FIXME comment confirms this was intended to be externalized.

The Coraza WAF rule engine is set to Off (SecRuleEngine Off), which disables all WAF rules including the SSTI, SQL injection, XSS, and path traversal protections defined on lines 17-20. This means the application receives all malicious payloads unfiltered, making the documented WAF rules completely ineffective.

The FastAPI debug flag is set to config.DEBUG, which reads from the DEBUG environment variable (app/config.py:9). When DEBUG=True, FastAPI exposes detailed error tracebacks including source code snippets and local variables, which can leak sensitive information. The .env_temp template file enables debug mode by default (DEBUG=True).
